title.zh: 卷三十二 樊宏陰識列傳
title.en: Volume 32: Biographies of Fan Hong; Yin Shi
title.raw: 卷三十二 樊宏陰識列傳 Volume 32: Biographies of Fan Hong; Yin Shi

sentences.zh: 樊宏字靡卿，南陽湖陽人也，世祖之舅。

translations.literal: Fan Hong, courtesy name Miqqing, was a man of Huyang in Nanyang; he was Shizu's maternal uncle.
translations.idiomatic: Fan Hong, courtesy name Miqqing, came from Huyang in Nanyang and was Emperor Guangwu's uncle by marriage.

sentences.zh: 其先周仲山甫，封於樊，因而氏焉，為鄉里著姓。

translations.literal: His ancestor was Zhou Zhongshanfu, enfeoffed at Fan, and from this took the surname; they became a prominent clan of the village.
translations.idiomatic: The line descended from Zhong Shanfu of Zhou, enfeoffed at Fan, whence the surname—a leading local family.

sentences.zh: 父重，字君雲，世善農稼，好貨殖。

translations.literal: His father Chong, courtesy name Junyun, for generations was good at farming and liked trade and accumulation.
translations.idiomatic: His father Chong, courtesy name Junyun, came from a line of skilled farmers and had a gift for commerce.

sentences.zh: 重性溫厚，有法度，三世共財，子孫朝夕禮敬，常若公家。

translations.literal: Chong's nature was mild and generous; he had standards and measures; three generations shared property; sons and grandsons morning and evening observed ritual respect, always as if in a public household.
translations.idiomatic: Chong was gentle and principled. Three generations held property in common; descendants greeted one another morning and evening with the formality of a court household.

sentences.zh: 其營理產業，物無所棄，課役童隸，各得其宜，故能上下戮力，財利歲倍，至乃開廣田土三百餘頃。

translations.literal: In managing his estates he wasted nothing; he assigned tasks to boys and slaves, each getting what was fitting; therefore above and below could join strength, profits doubled yearly, until he had opened and extended over three hundred qing of fields.
translations.idiomatic: He ran his lands without waste, matched every servant to the right task, and doubled his income year by year until he held more than three hundred qing.

sentences.zh: 其所起廬舍，皆有重堂高閣，陂渠灌註。

translations.literal: The lodges and houses he built all had double halls and high towers, ponds and channels for irrigation.
translations.idiomatic: His manor houses had double halls and tall pavilions, with dikes and canals for irrigation.

sentences.zh: 又池魚牧畜，有求必給。

translations.literal: Moreover pond fish and pastured livestock—whatever was sought was always supplied.
translations.idiomatic: Fishponds and herds supplied every request.

sentences.zh: 嘗欲作器物，先種梓漆，時人嗤之，然積以歲月，皆得其用，向之笑者咸求假焉。

translations.literal: When he wished to make tools and vessels, he first planted catalpa and lacquer; people of the time scoffed, yet accumulated over years, all obtained their use; those who had laughed all sought to borrow.
translations.idiomatic: Planning to furnish his estate, he planted catalpa and lacquer years ahead; neighbors mocked him until the timber and lacquer matured—then they all came begging to borrow.

sentences.zh: 資至巨萬，而賑贍宗族，恩加鄉閭。

translations.literal: His wealth reached tens of millions, yet he relieved and supplied the clan lineage, and grace extended to the village lanes.
translations.idiomatic: His fortune ran to millions, yet he fed his kin and spread generosity through the countryside.

sentences.zh: 外孫何氏兄弟爭財，重恥之，以田二頃解其忿訟。

translations.literal: His grandsons by his daughter, the He brothers, disputed property; Chong shamed at it, with two qing of fields resolved their angry lawsuit.
translations.idiomatic: When his He grandsons quarreled over an inheritance, Chong ended the suit with two qing of land out of shame at the dispute.

sentences.zh: 縣中稱美，推為三老。

translations.literal: The county praised his beauty and pushed him to be one of the three elders.
translations.idiomatic: The county honored him and named him one of the three village elders.

sentences.zh: 年八十餘終。

translations.literal: He ended at over eighty years of age.
translations.idiomatic: He died in his eighties.

sentences.zh: 其素所假貸人間數百萬，遺令焚削文契。

translations.literal: What he had habitually lent to others among the people was several million cash; he left instructions to burn and cut the written contracts.
translations.idiomatic: He had lent millions across the neighborhood; his dying order was to burn the IOUs.

sentences.zh: 責家聞者皆慚，爭往償之，諸子從敕，竟不肯受。

translations.literal: Creditors who heard were all ashamed and vied to go repay; his sons followed the command and to the end refused to accept.
translations.idiomatic: Debtors, shamed, rushed to repay; his sons obeyed his will and refused every coin.

sentences.zh: 宏少有誌行。

translations.literal: Hong in youth had aims and conduct.
translations.idiomatic: Fan Hong showed purpose and integrity from an early age.

sentences.zh: 王莽末，義兵起，劉伯升與族兄賜俱將兵攻湖陽，城守不下。

translations.literal: At the end of Wang Mang, righteous troops rose; Liu Bosheng together with his clan elder brother Ci both led troops to attack Huyang; the city held and would not fall.
translations.idiomatic: Late in Wang Mang's reign, Liu Yan (Bosheng) and his cousin Liu Ci besieged Huyang without taking it.

sentences.zh: 賜女弟為宏妻，湖陽由是收系宏妻子，令出譬伯升，宏因留不反。

translations.literal: Ci's younger sister was Hong's wife; Huyang thereupon seized and bound Hong's wife and children, ordered him to go out and persuade Bosheng; Hong thereupon stayed and did not return.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Ci's sister was Fan Hong's wife, so Huyang jailed Hong's family and ordered him to persuade Liu Yan to lift the siege. Hong stayed with the rebels instead.

sentences.zh: 湖陽軍帥欲殺其妻子，長吏以下共相謂曰：『樊重子父，禮義恩德行於鄉里，雖有罪，且當在後。

translations.literal: The Huyang army commander wished to kill his wife and children; the senior clerk downward all said to one another, 'Fan Chong's son and father—ritual, righteousness, kindness, and virtue move through the village lanes; though there is guilt, it should wait for later.'
translations.idiomatic: The garrison commander wanted them executed, but local officials said, 'The Fan family has earned the district's respect—punishment can wait.'

sentences.zh: 』會漢兵日盛，湖陽惶急，未敢殺之，遂得免脫。

translations.literal: It happened that Han troops grew stronger daily; Huyang was alarmed and urgent and did not dare kill them; thereupon they obtained release.
translations.idiomatic: As Han forces grew, Huyang panicked and never carried out the execution; the family went free.

sentences.zh: 更始立，欲以宏為將，宏叩頭辭曰：『書生不習兵事。

translations.literal: When Gengshi was established, he wished to make Hong a general; Hong knocked his head and declined, saying, 'A scholar does not practice military affairs.'
translations.idiomatic: The Gengshi regime wanted Fan Hong as a general; he kowtowed and refused: 'I am a scholar, not a soldier.'

sentences.zh: 』竟得免歸。

translations.literal: In the end he obtained dismissal and return.
translations.idiomatic: He was allowed to go home.

sentences.zh: 與宗家親屬作營塹自守，老弱歸之者千餘家。

translations.literal: Together with lineage kin he made ramparts and moats to defend himself; old and weak who came to him were over a thousand households.
translations.idiomatic: He and his clan threw up earthworks for defense; more than a thousand households of refugees joined him.

sentences.zh: 時赤眉賊掠唐子鄉，多所殘殺，欲前攻宏營，宏遣人持牛酒米穀，勞遺赤眉。

translations.literal: At that time the Red Eyebrow bandits plundered Tangzi township, killing many; they wished to advance and attack Hong's camp; Hong sent men bearing cattle, wine, rice, and grain to comfort and gift the Red Eyebrows.
translations.idiomatic: When Red Eyebrows ravaged Tangzi and turned toward Fan Hong's camp, he sent gifts of food and drink.

sentences.zh: 赤眉長老先聞宏仁厚，皆稱曰：『樊君素善，且今見待如此，何心攻之。

translations.literal: The Red Eyebrow elders had earlier heard Hong was benevolent and generous; they all declared, 'Lord Fan has always been good, and now we are treated thus—what heart would attack him?'
translations.idiomatic: Their elders had heard of Fan Hong's kindness. 'He has always been good to people, and now he treats us like this—how could we attack him?'

sentences.zh: 』引兵而去，遂免寇難。

translations.literal: They led troops and departed; thereupon he escaped bandit calamity.
translations.idiomatic: They marched away, and Fan Hong's people were spared.

sentences.zh: 世祖即位，拜光祿大夫，位特進，次三公。

translations.literal: When Shizu ascended the throne, he appointed Hong minister of the household, rank specially advanced, next after the three dukes.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Guangwu named him minister of the household with specially advanced rank, standing just below the three dukes.

sentences.zh: 建武五年，封長羅侯。

translations.literal: In the fifth year of Jianwu, he was enfeoffed as marquis of Changluo.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 5 he received the marquisate of Changluo.

sentences.zh: 十三年，封弟丹為射陽侯，兄子尋玄鄉侯，族兄忠更父侯。

translations.literal: In the thirteenth year, his younger brother Dan was enfeoffed as marquis of Sheyang, his elder brother's son Xun as marquis of Xuan township, his clan elder brother Zhong as marquis of Gengfu.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 13 his brother Dan became marquis of Sheyang, his nephew Xun marquis of Xuan township, and his cousin Zhong marquis of Gengfu.

sentences.zh: 十五年，定封宏壽張侯。

translations.literal: In the fifteenth year, Hong's enfeoffment was fixed as marquis of Shouzhang.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 15 his fief was fixed at Shouzhang.

sentences.zh: 十八年，帝南祠章陵，過湖陽，祠重墓，追爵謚為壽張敬侯，立廟於湖陽，車駕每南巡，常幸其墓，賞賜大會。

translations.literal: In the eighteenth year, the emperor sacrificed south at Zhangling, passed Huyang, sacrificed at Chong's tomb, posthumously enfeoffed and posthumously titled him reverent marquis of Shouzhang, established a temple at Huyang; whenever the imperial carriage toured south, he always favored his tomb with great grants and a great assembly.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 18 the emperor sacrificed at Zhangling, stopped at Huyang to honor Fan Chong's grave, posthumously titled him Reverent Marquis of Shouzhang, built a temple, and on every southern tour visited the tomb with rich gifts.

sentences.zh: 宏為人謙柔畏慎，不求茍進。

translations.literal: Hong as a person was humble, soft, fearful, and cautious; he did not seek careless advancement.
translations.idiomatic: Fan Hong was modest, cautious, and never grasped for promotion.

sentences.zh: 常戒其子曰：『富貴盈溢，未有能終者。

translations.literal: He often warned his sons, saying, 'Wealth and honor overflowing—none have been able to end well.'
translations.idiomatic: He often told his sons, 'Fortune that overflows its banks never lasts.'

sentences.zh: 吾非不喜榮勢也，天道惡滿而好謙，前世貴戚皆明戒也。

translations.literal: It is not that I do not like glory and power; Heaven's way hates fullness and likes humility; former generations' noble affines are all clear warnings.'
translations.idiomatic: I do not despise rank—Heaven hates the proud and loves the humble. The fate of every powerful clan is warning enough.'

sentences.zh: 保身全己，豈不樂哉！

translations.literal: To preserve the body and keep oneself whole—is that not joy!'
translations.idiomatic: To keep life and integrity whole—what greater joy is there?'

sentences.zh: 』每當朝會，輒迎期先到，俯伏待事，時至乃起。

translations.literal: Whenever there was court assembly, he always welcomed the appointed time by arriving first, prostrated waiting for business, and only when the time came did he rise.
translations.idiomatic: At every court session he arrived before the hour, lay prostrate until proceedings began, and only then rose.

sentences.zh: 帝聞之，常敕騶騎臨朝乃告，勿令豫到。

translations.literal: The emperor heard it and always ordered the outriders to inform only when approaching court, not letting him arrive in advance.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor told his escort to notify Fan Hong only at the last moment so he would not wait so long.

sentences.zh: 宏所上便宜及言得失，輒手自書寫，毀削草本。

translations.literal: What Hong submitted as expedient and spoke of gain and loss, he always personally wrote out with his hand, destroying and cutting the draft copies.
translations.idiomatic: He wrote his own memorials on policy and destroyed every draft.

sentences.zh: 公朝訪逮，不敢眾對。

translations.literal: At public court when questioned and summoned, he did not dare reply in the crowd.
translations.idiomatic: When questioned in open court he never answered in front of the assembly.

sentences.zh: 宗族染其化，未嘗犯法。

translations.literal: The clan lineage was dyed by his transformation; they never violated the law.
translations.idiomatic: His kin absorbed his example and never broke the law.

sentences.zh: 帝甚重之。

translations.literal: The emperor greatly valued him.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor held him in the highest regard.

sentences.zh: 及病困，車駕臨視，留宿，問其所欲言。

translations.literal: When he was gravely ill, the imperial carriage came to look in on him, stayed overnight, and asked what he wished to say.
translations.idiomatic: When he fell mortally ill, the emperor visited, stayed the night, and asked his last wishes.

sentences.zh: 宏頓首自陳：『無功享食大國，誠恐子孫不能保全厚恩，令臣魂神慚負黃泉，願還壽張，食小鄉亭。

translations.literal: Hong knocked his head and stated himself: 'Without merit I enjoy eating a great state; I truly fear sons and grandsons cannot preserve the thick grace, making your servant's soul ashamed before the yellow springs—I beg to return Shouzhang and eat from a small village and pavilion.'
translations.idiomatic: Fan Hong kowtowed and said, 'I have no merit yet hold a great fief; I fear my heirs cannot bear so much favor and my spirit would be shamed in the grave. Let me return Shouzhang for a minor fief.'

sentences.zh: 』帝悲傷其言，而竟不許。

translations.literal: The emperor grieved at his words, but in the end did not permit it.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor was moved but refused.

sentences.zh: 二十七年，卒。

translations.literal: In the twenty-seventh year, he died.
translations.idiomatic: He died in Jianwu 27.

sentences.zh: 遺敕薄葬，一無所用，以為棺柩一臧，不宜復見，如有腐敗，傷孝子之心，使與夫人同墳異臧。

translations.literal: He left orders for a thin burial, using nothing, holding that the inner coffin is one hidden thing, not fit to be seen again; if there is rot and decay, it wounds the filial son's heart—have him share a grave with the lady but separate inner coffins.
translations.idiomatic: He ordered the plainest burial: no grave goods. A coffin should not be reopened; decay would wound his sons' hearts. Bury him beside his wife in one mound but separate vaults.

sentences.zh: 帝善其令，以書示百官，因曰：『今不順壽張侯意，無以彰其德。

translations.literal: The emperor approved his command, showed the document to the hundred officials, and said, 'If now we do not follow the marquis of Shouzhang's intent, there is no way to display his virtue.'
translations.idiomatic: The emperor praised the testament, read it to the court, and said, 'We must honor the marquis of Shouzhang or fail his virtue.'

sentences.zh: 且吾萬歲之後，欲以為式。

translations.literal: Moreover after my myriad years, I wish to take it as a model.'
translations.idiomatic: When my own end comes, I want the same simplicity.'

sentences.zh: 』賻錢千萬，布萬匹，謚為恭侯，贈以印綬，車駕親送葬。

translations.literal: Condolence cash a thousand ten-thousands, cloth ten thousand bolts, posthumous title reverent marquis, granted seals and ribbons, the imperial carriage personally escorted the burial.
translations.idiomatic: The court granted ten million cash and ten thousand bolts of silk, titled him Reverent Marquis, bestowed seals, and the emperor attended the funeral.

sentences.zh: 子鯈嗣。

translations.literal: His son Tiao succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: His son Fan Tiao inherited the title.

sentences.zh: 帝悼宏不已，復封少子茂為平望侯。

translations.literal: The emperor mourned Hong unceasingly and again enfeoffed the younger son Mao as marquis of Pingwang.
translations.idiomatic: Grieving for Fan Hong, the emperor also enfeoffed his youngest son Fan Mao as marquis of Pingwang.

sentences.zh: 樊氏侯者凡五國。

translations.literal: Those of the Fan clan who were marquises were in all five states.
translations.idiomatic: Five branches of the Fan family held marquisates.

sentences.zh: 明年，賜鯈弟鮪及從昆弟七人合錢五千萬。

translations.literal: The next year, he granted Tiao's younger brother Wei and seven cousins-brother in all fifty million cash.
translations.idiomatic: The following year the court gave Fan Wei and seven cousins fifty million cash.

sentences.zh: 論曰：昔楚頃襄王問陽陵君曰：『君子之富何如？

translations.literal: The treatise says: Formerly King Qingxiang of Chu asked the lord of Yangling, saying, 'What is the noble man's wealth like?'
translations.idiomatic: The historian remarks: King Qingxiang of Chu once asked the lord of Yangling what a gentleman's wealth should be.

sentences.zh: 』對曰：『假人不德不責，食人不使不役，親戚愛之，眾人善之。

translations.literal: He replied, 'Lending to people, he does not demand virtue nor press for repayment; feeding people, he does not employ nor conscript; kin love him, the crowd praises him.'
translations.idiomatic: The answer: 'He lends without hounding debtors, feeds people without turning them into servants, wins love from kin and praise from all.'

sentences.zh: 』若乃樊重之折契止訟，其庶幾君子之富乎！

translations.literal: As for Fan Chong's breaking contracts to stop lawsuits—perhaps it approaches the noble man's wealth!'
translations.idiomatic: Fan Chong's burning of IOUs to end quarrels comes close to that ideal.'

sentences.zh: 分地以用天道，實廩以崇禮節，取諸理化，則亦可以施於政也。

translations.literal: Dividing land to employ Heaven's way, filling granaries to honor ritual and regulation—taken from these principles of transformation, then they can also be applied in government.'
translations.idiomatic: Heaven works through sharing; full granaries uphold ritual. Apply that wisdom, and it becomes sound government.'

sentences.zh: 與夫愛而畏者，何殊間哉！

translations.literal: Compared to those who are loved yet feared—how different is the interval!'
translations.idiomatic: How far that stands from wealth that buys fear as well as love!'

sentences.zh: 鯈字長魚，謹約有父風。

translations.literal: Tiao, courtesy name Changyu, was cautious and restrained and had his father's manner.
translations.idiomatic: Fan Tiao, courtesy name Changyu, was as careful and restrained as his father.

sentences.zh: 事後母至孝，及母卒，哀思過禮，毀病不自支，世祖常遣中黃門朝暮送饘粥。

translations.literal: He served his stepmother with utmost filiality; when she died, grief and longing exceeded the rites; he wasted away ill and could not support himself; Shizu often sent palace attendants morning and evening to deliver gruel.
translations.idiomatic: He mourned his stepmother past the bounds of ritual until he fell ill; Emperor Guangwu sent eunuchs morning and evening with gruel.

sentences.zh: 服闋，就侍中丁恭受《公羊嚴氏春秋》。

translations.literal: When mourning was complete, he went to attendant Ding Gong to receive the Gongyang Yan lineage Spring and Autumn Annals.
translations.idiomatic: After mourning he studied the Yan school Gongyang commentary on the Spring and Autumn Annals under Attendant-in-Chief Ding Gong.

sentences.zh: 建武中，禁網尚闊，諸王既長，各招引賓客，以鯈外戚，爭遣致之，而鯈清靜自保，無所交結。

translations.literal: In the Jianwu period, the prohibition net was still loose; the various princes having grown, each summoned and drew retainers; because Tiao was outer kin, they vied to send and bring him, yet Tiao was clear and quiet, preserving himself, associating and connecting with none.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu the laws were loose; grown princes vied for clients and courted Fan Tiao as imperial in-law, but he kept to himself and joined no faction.

sentences.zh: 及沛王輔事發，貴戚子弟多見收捕，鯈以不豫得免。

translations.literal: When the Prince of Pei Fu affair broke, noble affines' sons and younger kin were mostly seized and arrested; Tiao because he had not participated obtained exemption.
translations.idiomatic: When Prince Liu Fu of Pei fell, many imperial in-laws were arrested; Fan Tiao escaped because he had never joined their circle.

sentences.zh: 帝崩，鯈為復土校尉。

translations.literal: When the emperor died, Tiao became colonel of the earth-returning [burial].
translations.idiomatic: When the emperor died, Fan Tiao served as colonel in charge of the imperial burial.

sentences.zh: 十年，鯈卒，赗贈甚厚，謚曰哀侯。

translations.literal: In the tenth year, Tiao died; burial gifts were very generous; posthumous title was marquis of pity.
translations.idiomatic: In the tenth year of Yongping he died with rich funeral honors and the posthumous title Pitiful Marquis.

sentences.zh: 帝遣小黃門張音問所遺言。

translations.literal: The emperor sent minor palace attendant Zhang Yin to ask his dying words.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor sent the eunuch Zhang Yin to record his last requests.

sentences.zh: 先是河南縣亡失官錢，典負者坐死及罪徙者甚眾，遂委責於人，以償其耗。

translations.literal: Earlier Henan county had lost official cash; those in charge of the debt by offense sat for death and those by crime exiled were very many; thereupon they shifted blame onto others to repay the loss.
translations.idiomatic: Henan county had lost treasury funds; many clerks faced death or exile, and blame was passed down the line to cover the shortfall.

sentences.zh: 鄉部吏司因此為奸，鯈常疾之。

translations.literal: Village department clerks for this reason practiced villainy; Tiao always hated it.
translations.idiomatic: Rural officials turned the mess to their profit; Fan Tiao despised it.

sentences.zh: 又野王歲獻甘醪、膏餳，每輒擾人，吏以為利。

translations.literal: Moreover Yewang yearly presented sweet ale and malt sugar; each time it disturbed the people; clerks took it as profit.
translations.idiomatic: Yewang's annual tribute of ale and malt candy harassed the people while clerks skimmed the profit.

sentences.zh: 鯈並欲奏罷之，疾病未及得上。

translations.literal: Tiao also wished to memorialize to abolish them; illness came before he could submit upward.
translations.idiomatic: Fan Tiao meant to abolish both practices but fell ill before he could memorialize.

sentences.zh: 音歸，具以聞，帝覽之而悲嘆，敕二郡並令從之。

translations.literal: Yin returned, reported it all in full; the emperor read it and sighed in grief, ordered the two commanderies together to order compliance with it.
translations.idiomatic: Zhang Yin reported everything; the emperor read Fan Tiao's words with grief and ordered both commanderies to comply.

sentences.zh: 長子汜嗣，以次子郴、梵為郎。

translations.literal: The eldest son Si succeeded; the second sons Chen and Fan were made gentlemen.
translations.idiomatic: The eldest son Fan Si inherited; younger sons Chen and Fan became gentlemen of the palace.

sentences.zh: 其後楚事發覺，帝追念鯈謹恪，又聞其止鮪婚事，故其諸子得不坐焉。

translations.literal: Later when the Chu affair was discovered, the emperor recalled Tiao's careful reverence, and also heard that he had stopped Wei's marriage affair; therefore his various sons were able not to be implicated.
translations.idiomatic: When the Chu conspiracy broke, the emperor remembered Fan Tiao's caution and his refusal of Fan Wei's marriage scheme, and spared his sons.

sentences.zh: 梵字文高，為郎二十餘年，三署服其重懼。

translations.literal: Fan, courtesy name Wengao, was a gentleman over twenty years; the three bureaus submitted to his solemn fearfulness.
translations.idiomatic: Fan Fan, courtesy name Wengao, served more than twenty years as a gentleman; all three palace bureaus respected his scrupulous gravity.

sentences.zh: 悉推財物二千餘萬與孤兄子，官至大鴻臚。

translations.literal: He pushed all property over twenty million to his orphaned elder brother's son; his office rose to grand herald.
translations.idiomatic: He gave over twenty million in property to a nephew who had lost his father and rose to grand herald.

sentences.zh: 汜卒，子時嗣。

translations.literal: When Si died, his son Shi succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: When Fan Si died, his son Fan Shi inherited.

sentences.zh: 時卒，子建嗣。

translations.literal: When Shi died, his son Jian succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: When Fan Shi died, his son Fan Jian inherited.

sentences.zh: 建卒，無子，國絕。

translations.literal: When Jian died, there was no son; the state was cut off.
translations.idiomatic: Fan Jian died without an heir and the marquisate lapsed.

sentences.zh: 永寧元年，鄧太后復封建弟盼。

translations.literal: In the first year of Yongning, Empress Dowager Deng again enfeoffed Jian's younger brother Pan.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongning 1 Empress Dowager Deng restored the fief to Fan Jian's brother Fan Pan.

sentences.zh: 盼卒，子尚嗣。

translations.literal: When Pan died, his son Shang succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: When Fan Pan died, his son Fan Shang inherited.

sentences.zh: 初，鯈刪定《公羊嚴氏春秋》章句，世號『樊侯學』，教授門徒前後三千餘人。

translations.literal: Earlier, Tiao edited and fixed the Gongyang Yan lineage Spring and Autumn Annals sentence sections; the age titled it 'marquis of Fan learning'; he taught disciples before and after over three thousand persons.
translations.idiomatic: Fan Tiao had edited the Yan school Gongyang commentary; the age called it Marquis Fan's school, and he taught more than three thousand students.

sentences.zh: 弟子潁川李脩、九江夏勤，皆為三公。

translations.literal: Disciples Li Xiu of Yingchuan and Xia Qin of Jiujiang both became three dukes.
translations.idiomatic: His pupils Li Xiu of Yingchuan and Xia Qin of Jiujiang rose to the three dukes.

sentences.zh: 勤字伯宗，為京、宛二縣令，零陵太守，所在有理能稱。

translations.literal: Qin, courtesy name Bozong, was magistrate of the two counties Jing and Wan, governor of Lingling; wherever he was there was praise for administrative ability.
translations.idiomatic: Xia Qin, courtesy name Bozong, governed Jing and Wan counties and Lingling commandery, earning a name for competence wherever he served.

sentences.zh: 安帝時，位至司徒。

translations.literal: Under Emperor An, his position reached minister of education.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor An he became minister of education.

sentences.zh: 族曾孫準

translations.literal: Clan great-grandson Zhun (section heading).
translations.idiomatic: Fan Zhun, a descendant of the clan (section title).

sentences.zh: 準字幼陵，宏之族曾孫也。

translations.literal: Zhun, courtesy name Youling, was Hong's clan great-grandson.
translations.idiomatic: Fan Zhun, courtesy name Youling, was a great-grandnephew of Fan Hong's line.

sentences.zh: 父瑞，好黃老言，清靜少欲。

translations.literal: His father Rui liked Huang-Lao doctrines, was clear and quiet with few desires.
translations.idiomatic: His father Rui followed Huang-Lao teaching and lived simply with few wants.

sentences.zh: 準少勵志行，修儒術，以先父產業數百萬讓孤兄子。

translations.literal: Zhun in youth urged aims and conduct, cultivated Confucian arts, and yielded his late father's property of several million to his orphaned elder brother's son.
translations.idiomatic: In youth Fan Zhun studied Confucian learning and gave his late father's estate, worth millions, to a fatherless nephew.

sentences.zh: 永元十五年，和帝幸南陽，準為郡功曹，召見，帝器之，拜郎中，從車駕還宮，特補尚書郎。

translations.literal: In the fifteenth year of Yongyuan, Emperor He favored Nanyang; Zhun was commandery merit clerk; summoned to audience, the emperor valued him, appointed him gentleman of the palace, followed the imperial carriage returning to the palace, specially supplemented as secretary.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongyuan 15 Emperor He toured Nanyang. Fan Zhun, merit clerk of the commandery, so impressed the emperor that he was named gentleman of the palace and, after escorting the court home, was specially appointed secretary.

sentences.zh: 鄧太后臨朝，儒學陵替，準乃上疏曰：

translations.literal: When Empress Dowager Deng faced the court, Confucian learning declined and fell; Zhun thereupon submitted a memorial saying:
translations.idiomatic: When Empress Dowager Deng ruled from behind the screen, classical learning declined; Fan Zhun memorialized:

sentences.zh: 臣聞賈誼有言，『人君不可以不學』。

translations.literal: Your servant has heard Jia Yi had words: 'The lord of men cannot but study.'
translations.idiomatic: Jia Yi said, 'A ruler must never stop learning.'

sentences.zh: 故雖大舜聖德，孳孳為善；

translations.literal: Therefore though great Shun had sagely virtue, he was zealous in doing good;'
translations.idiomatic: Even Shun, for all his sagely virtue, strove endlessly for goodness;'

sentences.zh: 成王賢主，崇明師傅。

translations.literal: King Cheng was a worthy lord; he exalted and clarified tutors and masters.'
translations.idiomatic: King Cheng was a wise king who honored his teachers.'

sentences.zh: 及光武皇帝受命中興，群雄崩擾，旌旗亂野，東西誅戰，不遑啟處，然猶投戈講藝，息馬論道。

translations.literal: When Emperor Guangwu received the mandate and revived the house, the host of heroes collapsed in turmoil, banners and flags maddened the wilderness, east and west punished and battled, no leisure to kneel or sit—yet still he cast aside the spear and lectured on arts, rested horses and discussed the Way.'
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Guangwu raised the Han amid chaos and war, yet still laid down arms to teach the classics and dismounted to discuss the Way.'

sentences.zh: 至孝明皇帝，兼天地之姿，用日月之明，庶政萬機，無不簡心，而垂情古典，遊意經藝，每饗射禮畢，正坐自講，諸儒並聽，四方欣欣。

translations.literal: Coming to Emperor Xiaoming, combining Heaven and Earth's bearing, employing sun and moon's brightness—myriad affairs of the myriad tasks, none not simplified in heart—yet he let feeling hang on classical antiquity, roamed intent in the classics and arts; each time the banquet archery rite finished, he sat upright and lectured himself; the various scholars all listened; the four quarters were joyful.'
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Ming combined natural brilliance with care for every duty, yet loved the classics: after each archery rite he lectured the erudites himself, and the realm rejoiced.'

sentences.zh: 雖闕里之化，矍相之事，誠不足言。

translations.literal: Though the transformation of Que village, the affairs of Juexiang—truly not worth speaking of.'
translations.idiomatic: Confucius's school at Que and the archery grounds at Juexiang hardly compare.'

sentences.zh: 又多徵名儒，以充禮官，如沛國趙孝、瑯邪承宮等，或安車結駟，告歸鄉里；

translations.literal: Moreover many were summoned, famous scholars, to fill ritual offices, such as Zhao Xiao of Pei state, Cheng Gong of Langye, and others—some with secure carriages and four-horse teams, announcing return to village lanes;'
translations.idiomatic: Famous scholars such as Zhao Xiao of Pei and Cheng Gong of Langye filled ritual posts—some rode home in honor carriages;'

sentences.zh: 或豐衣博帶，從見宗廟。

translations.literal: some in rich robes and broad belts, following to see the ancestral temple.'
translations.idiomatic: others in full dress attended the imperial shrines.'

sentences.zh: 其餘以經術見優者，布在廊廟。

translations.literal: The rest who by classic arts were shown favor were spread through hall and temple.'
translations.idiomatic: Every scholar who excelled in the classics held a place at court.'

sentences.zh: 故朝多皤皤之良，華首之老。

translations.literal: Therefore the court had many hoary-headed worthies, flower-headed elders.'
translations.idiomatic: The court thronged with white-haired worthies.'

sentences.zh: 每宴會，則論難衎衎，共求政化。

translations.literal: Whenever there was banquet assembly, then they debated difficulties with harmonious ease, jointly seeking government and transformation.'
translations.idiomatic: At every feast they debated the classics in good humor, all seeking sound government.'

sentences.zh: 詳覽群言，響如振玉。

translations.literal: He examined in detail the host of words; the echo was like shaking jade.
translations.idiomatic: He weighed every opinion; their resonance was like the chime of jade.

sentences.zh: 朝者進而思政，罷者退而備問。

translations.literal: Those who courted advanced and pondered governance; those who left office withdrew and prepared questions.
translations.idiomatic: Men at court thought on policy; men who left office studied to ask better questions.

sentences.zh: 小大隨化，雍雍可嘉。

translations.literal: Small and great followed transformation; harmonious, worthy of praise.
translations.idiomatic: High and low moved with the civilizing tide—harmony worth praising.

sentences.zh: 期門羽林介胄之士，悉通《孝經》。

translations.literal: Gentlemen of the palace gate and feathered forest in armor and helmet—all comprehended the Classic of Filial Piety.
translations.idiomatic: Even the armored guards of the palace gates and feathered forest knew the Classic of Filial Piety.

sentences.zh: 博士議郎，一人開門，徒眾百數。

translations.literal: Erudites and gentleman consultants—one man opening a gate, followers numbering in the hundreds.
translations.idiomatic: Each erudite or consultant who opened a school drew hundreds of disciples.

sentences.zh: 化自聖躬，流及蠻荒，匈奴遣伊秩訾王大車且渠來入就學。

translations.literal: Transformation came from the sage's person, flowed to the wild Man; the Xiongnu sent the Yizhi Zi king, great cart Juqu, to come in and attend study.
translations.idiomatic: Culture radiated from the throne to the barbarian marches—the Xiongnu even sent the Worthy King of the Left and senior chief Juqu to study at court.

sentences.zh: 八方肅清，上下無事。

translations.literal: The eight directions were cleared and pure; above and below had no troubles.
translations.idiomatic: The realm was at peace from every quarter; court and country were untroubled.

sentences.zh: 是以議者每稱盛時，咸言永平。

translations.literal: Therefore discussers whenever they praised a flourishing age all spoke of Yongping.
translations.idiomatic: Critics who praise a golden age always name the Yongping years.

sentences.zh: 今學者蓋少，遠方尤甚。

translations.literal: Now scholars are perhaps few; the distant regions are especially severe.
translations.idiomatic: Today scholars are scarce, and the frontier regions worst of all.

sentences.zh: 博士倚席不講，儒者竟論浮麗，忘謇謇之忠，習諓々之辭。

translations.literal: Erudites lean on the mat and do not lecture; Confucians compete in discussing floating splendor, forget outspoken loyalty, practice slanderous phrases.
translations.idiomatic: Erudites no longer teach; scholars chase flashy rhetoric, abandon blunt loyalty, and trade in slick phrases.

sentences.zh: 文吏則去法律而學詆欺，銳錐刀之鋒，斷刑辟之重，德陋俗薄，以致苛刻。

translations.literal: Document clerks then leave law and statutes and study slander and deceit, sharpen awl-and-knife points, judge the weight of punishments and executions—virtue base, customs thin, thereby bringing harsh severity.
translations.idiomatic: Clerical officials ignore the code and learn petty tricks; they sharpen quibbles over the death penalty—mean virtue, thin custom, and cruel judgments follow.

sentences.zh: 昔孝文竇後性好黃老，而清靜之化流景、武之間。

translations.literal: Formerly Empress Dou of Emperor Wen's nature liked Huang-Lao, yet the transformation of clarity and quiet flowed between Jing and Wu.
translations.idiomatic: Empress Dou under Emperor Wen loved Huang-Lao, yet her quiet influence lasted into the reigns of Emperors Jing and Wu.

sentences.zh: 臣愚以為宜下明詔，博求幽隱，發揚巖穴，寵進儒雅，有如孝、宮者，徵詣公車，以侯聖上講習之期。

translations.literal: Your servant foolishly thinks it fitting to issue a clear edict, broadly seek the hidden and concealed, raise up cliff and cave, favor and advance Confucian elegance—such as Xiao and Gong—summon them to the public carriage, to await the sage superior's period of lecturing and practice.'
translations.idiomatic: Issue an edict summoning hidden scholars and men of learning like Zhao Xiao and Cheng Gong to the capital to await your lectures.'

sentences.zh: 公卿各舉明經及舊儒子孫，進其爵位，使纘其業。

translations.literal: Dukes and ministers each recommend those clear in the classics and old Confucians' descendants, advance their noble ranks, make them continue their enterprises.'
translations.idiomatic: Let every minister nominate classicists and heirs of old scholarly lines, raise their rank, and set them to carry on the tradition.'

sentences.zh: 復召郡國書佐，使讀律令。

translations.literal: Again summon commandery and kingdom document assistants, make them read statutes and orders.'
translations.idiomatic: Recall clerks from the commanderies to study the legal code.'

sentences.zh: 如此，則廷頸者日有所見，傾耳者月有所聞。

translations.literal: Thus, then those craning necks daily have something to see, those tilting ears monthly have something to hear.'
translations.idiomatic: Then those who strain to see and hear will daily and monthly find something worth learning.'

sentences.zh: 伏願陛下推述先帝進業之道。

translations.literal: Prostrate, I wish Your Majesty would extend and narrate the former emperors' way of advancing enterprise.'
translations.idiomatic: I beg you to follow the path by which former emperors advanced learning.'

sentences.zh: 太后深納其言，是後屢舉方正、敦樸、仁賢之士。

translations.literal: The empress dowager deeply accepted his words; after this she repeatedly recommended upright, honest, benevolent, and worthy men.
translations.idiomatic: The empress dowager took his advice to heart and thereafter often summoned men of integrity and benevolence.

sentences.zh: 準再遷御史中丞。

translations.literal: Zhun was transferred again to palace assistant to the imperial clerk.
translations.idiomatic: Fan Zhun was promoted to palace assistant to the imperial counselor.

sentences.zh: 永初之初，連年水旱災異，郡國多被饑困，準上疏曰：

translations.literal: At the beginning of Yongchu, successive years had flood, drought, and prodigies; commanderies and kingdoms mostly suffered famine and distress; Zhun submitted a memorial saying:
translations.idiomatic: Early in Yongchu, floods, droughts, and strange portents brought famine to the commanderies. Fan Zhun memorialized:

sentences.zh: 臣聞傳曰：『饑而不損茲曰太，厥災水。

translations.literal: Your servant has heard the tradition says: 'Famine without reducing this is called excess; its calamity is water.'
translations.idiomatic: The tradition says: 'When famine comes and the court does not retrench, that excess brings flood.'

sentences.zh: 』《春秋穀梁傳》曰：『五穀不登，謂之大侵。

translations.literal: The Guliang commentary on the Spring and Autumn Annals says: 'When the five grains do not ripen, it is called great invasion.'
translations.idiomatic: The Guliang commentary calls crop failure a great invasion.'

sentences.zh: 大侵之禮，百官備而不制，群神禱而不祠。

translations.literal: In the rites of great invasion, the hundred officials are complete but do not regulate; the host of spirits are prayed to but not sacrificed.'
translations.idiomatic: In such a crisis officials stand ready but suspend normal business; you pray to the spirits but hold no regular sacrifices.'

sentences.zh: 』由是言之，調和陰陽，實在儉節。

translations.literal: From this speaking, harmonizing yin and yang truly lies in frugality and restraint.'
translations.idiomatic: Harmony of yin and yang depends on austerity.'

sentences.zh: 朝廷雖勞心元元，事從省約，而在職之吏，尚未奉承。

translations.literal: Though the court toils its heart for the black-haired people and affairs follow reduction and economy, yet clerks in office have still not obeyed and carried out.
translations.idiomatic: The court cares for the people and orders frugality, but local officials still disobey.

sentences.zh: 夫建化致理，由近及遠，故《詩》曰『京師翼翼，四方是則』。

translations.literal: Now establishing transformation and reaching good order goes from near to far; therefore the Odes says, 'The capital is awe-inspiring; the four quarters take it as model.'
translations.idiomatic: Good government spreads from the center outward, as the Odes says: 'The capital sets the pattern for the realm.'

sentences.zh: 今可先令太官、尚方、考功、上林池籞諸官，實減無事之物，五府調省中都官吏京師作者。

translations.literal: Now one can first order the grand provisioner, imperial workshop, merit evaluation, Shanglin ponds and preserves, the various offices—truly reduce useless things; the five offices adjust and reduce capital clerks and capital artisans.
translations.idiomatic: Begin by cutting waste at the imperial kitchen, workshops, merit office, and Shanglin parks, and trim staff and artisans in the capital.

sentences.zh: 如此，則化及四方，人勞省息。

translations.literal: Thus, then transformation reaches the four quarters; people's labor is reduced and rests.'
translations.idiomatic: Then reform will reach the provinces and ease the people's burden.'

sentences.zh: 伏見被災之郡，百姓雕殘，恐非賑給所能勝贍，雖有其名，終無其實。

translations.literal: Prostrate, I see the stricken commanderies; the hundred surnames are carved and maimed—I fear relief grants cannot suffice to support; though they have the name, in the end they lack the reality.'
translations.idiomatic: The stricken districts are devastated; nominal relief cannot feed them in fact.'

sentences.zh: 可依徵和元年故事，遣使持節慰安。

translations.literal: One can follow the precedent of the first year of Zhenghe, send envoys bearing credentials to comfort and soothe.'
translations.idiomatic: Follow Emperor Wu's Zhenghe precedent: send credentialed envoys to comfort the people.'

sentences.zh: 尤困乏者，徙置荊、揚孰郡，既省轉運之費，且令百姓各安其所。

translations.literal: Those especially destitute, move and place them in Jing and Yang ripe commanderies—both saving transport costs and ordering the hundred surnames each to settle their place.
translations.idiomatic: Resettle the worst cases in the fertile Jing and Yang commanderies to cut transport costs and let people find stable homes.

sentences.zh: 今雖有西屯之役，宜先東州之急。

translations.literal: Now though there is the western garrison campaign, one ought first for the eastern provinces' urgency.'
translations.idiomatic: Even with the western campaign, the east's famine must come first.'

sentences.zh: 如遣使者與二千石隨事消息，悉留富人守其舊士，轉尤貧者過所衣食，誠父母之計也。

translations.literal: If envoys are sent with two-thousand-shi officials to handle matters as they arise, entirely keep rich men guarding their old lands, transfer the especially poor past what they eat and wear—it is truly a parent's plan.'
translations.idiomatic: Send envoys with governors to manage resettlement: keep the wealthy on their land, move only the destitute, and supply them on the road—that would be true parental care for the people.'

sentences.zh: 願以臣言下公卿平議。

translations.literal: I wish to take your servant's words and send them down to dukes and ministers for balanced discussion.'
translations.idiomatic: Submit these proposals to the ministers for debate.'

sentences.zh: 太后從之，悉以公田賦與貧人。

translations.literal: The empress dowager followed it; entirely gave public fields as grants to poor people.
translations.idiomatic: The empress dowager agreed and assigned public farmland to the poor.

sentences.zh: 即擢準與議郎呂倉並守光祿大夫，準使冀州，倉使兗州。

translations.literal: At once she promoted Zhun and gentleman consultant Lü Cang together to acting minister of the household; Zhun was sent to Ji province, Cang to Yan province.
translations.idiomatic: She immediately named Fan Zhun and Lü Cang acting ministers of the household and sent them to Ji and Yan provinces.

sentences.zh: 準到部，開倉廩食，尉安生業，流人咸得蘇息。

translations.literal: When Zhun reached his department, he opened granaries and fed them, comforted and settled livelihoods; wandering people all obtained revival and rest.
translations.idiomatic: Fan Zhun opened the granaries, fed the refugees, and helped them settle; wanderers found relief.

sentences.zh: 還，拜巨鹿太守。

translations.literal: Returning, he was appointed governor of Julu.
translations.idiomatic: On his return he became governor of Julu.

sentences.zh: 時饑荒之餘，人庶流迸，家戶且盡，準課督農桑，廣施方略，期年間，穀粟豐賤數十倍。

translations.literal: At that time after famine, people fled and scattered; households were nearly gone; Zhun assessed and supervised farming and sericulture, broadly applied strategy; within a full year, grain was abundant and cheap by several tens of times.
translations.idiomatic: After the famine, migration had emptied the district. Fan Zhun promoted farming; within a year grain was abundant and prices fell manyfold.

sentences.zh: 而趙、魏之郊數為羌所抄暴，準外禦寇虜，內撫百姓，郡境以安。

translations.literal: Moreover the outskirts of Zhao and Wei were repeatedly raided and ravaged by the Qiang; Zhun outwardly defended against bandits and barbarians, inwardly comforted the hundred surnames; the commandery border was thereby secure.
translations.idiomatic: While Qiang raiders plagued the Zhao-Wei border, he defended the frontier and soothed the people; Julu stayed calm.

sentences.zh: 五年，轉河內太守。

translations.literal: In the fifth year, he was transferred to governor of Henei.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongchu 5 he was transferred to governor of Henei.

sentences.zh: 時羌復屢入郡界，準輒將兵討逐，修理塢壁，威名大行。

translations.literal: At that time the Qiang again repeatedly entered the commandery border; Zhun always led troops to attack and drive them, repaired forts and walls; his awe-name spread greatly.
translations.idiomatic: When the Qiang raided again, he led troops to drive them off and rebuilt the stockades; his reputation spread.

sentences.zh: 視事三年，以疾徵，三轉為尚書令，明習故事，遂見任用。

translations.literal: After three years in office, he was summoned for illness; after three transfers he became director of the secretariat; he was clear and practiced in precedents; thereupon he was employed.
translations.idiomatic: After three years he was recalled on grounds of illness, then rose to director of the secretariat; his mastery of precedent won trust.

sentences.zh: 元初三年，代周暢為光祿勛。

translations.literal: In the third year of Yuanchu, he replaced Zhou Chang as superintendent of the palace.
translations.idiomatic: In Yuanchu 3 he succeeded Zhou Chang as superintendent of the palace.

sentences.zh: 五年，卒於官。

translations.literal: In the fifth year, he died in office.
translations.idiomatic: He died in office in the fifth year of Yuanchu.

sentences.zh: 陰識字次伯，南陽新野人也，光烈皇后之前母兄也。

translations.literal: Yin Shi, courtesy name Cibo, was a man of Xinye in Nanyang; he was the Empress Guanglie's elder uterine brother by her birth mother.
translations.idiomatic: Yin Shi, courtesy name Cibo, came from Xinye in Nanyang and was the elder brother of Empress Yin Lihua by the same mother.

sentences.zh: 其先出自管仲，管仲七世孫修，自齊適楚，為陰大夫，因而氏焉。

translations.literal: His ancestor came from Guan Zhong; Guan Zhong's seventh-generation descendant Xiu, from Qi went to Chu, became Yin grandee, and from this took the surname.
translations.idiomatic: The line descended from Guan Zhong; seven generations later Xiu left Qi for Chu, became lord Yin, and took Yin as the clan name.

sentences.zh: 秦、漢之際，始家新野。

translations.literal: At the juncture of Qin and Han, they first settled household at Xinye.
translations.idiomatic: At the fall of Qin they settled in Xinye.

sentences.zh: 及劉伯升起義兵，識時遊學長安，聞之，委業而歸，率子弟、宗族、賓客千餘人往詣伯升。

translations.literal: When Liu Bosheng raised righteous troops, Shi at that time was studying abroad in Chang'an; hearing it, he abandoned his enterprise and returned, leading sons and younger brothers, clan lineage, and guests over a thousand persons to go to Bosheng.
translations.idiomatic: When Liu Yan raised troops, Yin Shi was studying in Chang'an; he rushed home with more than a thousand kinsmen and retainers to join Liu Yan.

sentences.zh: 伯升乃以識為校尉。

translations.literal: Bosheng thereupon made Shi a colonel.
translations.idiomatic: Liu Yan named him a colonel.

sentences.zh: 更始元年，遷偏將軍，從攻宛，別降新野、淯陽、杜衍、冠軍、湖陽。

translations.literal: In the first year of Gengshi, he was promoted to lieutenant general, followed in attacking Wan, separately submitted Xinye, Yuyang, Duyan, Guanjun, Huyang.
translations.idiomatic: In Gengshi 1 he became lieutenant general, joined the attack on Wan, and separately brought Xinye, Yuyang, Duyan, Guanjun, and Huyang to surrender.

sentences.zh: 二年，更始封識陰德侯，行大將軍事。

translations.literal: In the second year, Gengshi enfeoffed Shi as marquis of Yinde, acting grand general affairs.
translations.idiomatic: In the second year the Gengshi court enfeoffed him as marquis of Yinde with the powers of grand general.

sentences.zh: 建武元年，光武遣使迎陰貴人於新野，並徵識。

translations.literal: In the first year of Jianwu, Guangwu sent envoys to welcome the Yin honorable lady at Xinye, and concurrently summoned Shi.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 1 Guangwu sent for Lady Yin from Xinye and summoned Yin Shi as well.

sentences.zh: 識隨貴人至，以為騎都尉，更封陰鄉侯。

translations.literal: Shi followed the honorable lady in arriving; he was made commandant of cavalry, transferred enfeoffment to marquis of Yin township.
translations.idiomatic: He accompanied his sister to court as commandant of cavalry and received a new fief as marquis of Yin township.

sentences.zh: 二年，以征伐軍功增封，識叩頭讓曰：『天下初定，將帥有功者眾，臣托屬掖廷，仍加爵邑，不可以示天下。

translations.literal: In the second year, for campaign and punitive merit his enfeoffment was increased; Shi knocked his head and yielded, saying, 'The realm is newly settled; commanders and generals with merit are many; your servant relies on connection to the rear palace, yet is again added noble rank and territory—it cannot be shown to the realm.'
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 2 his fief was enlarged for military merit; Yin Shi kowtowed and refused: 'The empire is new and many generals deserve reward; I am kin to the harem—more land for me would send the wrong message.'

sentences.zh: 』帝甚美之，以為關都尉，鎮函谷。

translations.literal: The emperor greatly praised him, made him colonel of the passes, garrisoned Hangu.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor admired his modesty and named him colonel of the passes to guard Hangu.

sentences.zh: 遷侍中，以母憂辭歸。

translations.literal: He was promoted to palace attendant; for mother's mourning he resigned and returned.
translations.idiomatic: He rose to palace attendant, then resigned to mourn his mother.

sentences.zh: 十五年，定封原鹿侯。

translations.literal: In the fifteenth year, his enfeoffment was fixed as marquis of Yuanlu.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 15 his fief was fixed at Yuanlu.

sentences.zh: 及顯宗立為皇太子，以識守執金吾，輔導東宮。

translations.literal: When Emperor Ming was established as heir apparent, Shi was made to guard the bearer of the mace and instruct and guide the eastern palace.
translations.idiomatic: When the future Emperor Ming became heir apparent, Yin Shi served as bearer of the mace and tutored the eastern palace.

sentences.zh: 帝每巡郡國，識常留鎮守京師，委以禁兵。

translations.literal: Whenever the emperor toured commanderies and kingdoms, Shi always remained to garrison and guard the capital, entrusting him with forbidden troops.
translations.idiomatic: Whenever the emperor toured the provinces, Yin Shi stayed to guard the capital and command the guards.

sentences.zh: 入雖極言正議，及與賓客語，未嘗及國事。

translations.literal: In entering though he spoke to the utmost correct views, when speaking with guests he never touched state affairs.
translations.idiomatic: He spoke frankly in audience but never discussed state business with visitors.

sentences.zh: 帝敬重之，常指識以敕戒貴戚，激厲左右焉。

translations.literal: The emperor respected and valued him, often pointed to Shi to instruct and warn noble affines, and to stimulate and encourage those around him.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor esteemed him and held him up as a model to imperial in-laws and the court.

sentences.zh: 識所用掾史皆簡賢者，如虞廷、傅寬、薛愔等，多至公卿校尉。

translations.literal: The clerks Shi employed were all selected worthies, such as Yu Ting, Fu Kuan, Xue Yin, and others; many reached three dukes and colonels.
translations.idiomatic: He chose worthy subordinates such as Yu Ting, Fu Kuan, and Xue Yin; many rose to ministerial rank.

sentences.zh: 顯宗即位，拜為執金吾，位特進。

translations.literal: When Emperor Ming ascended the throne, he was appointed bearer of the mace, rank specially advanced.
translations.idiomatic: When Emperor Ming took the throne, Yin Shi became bearer of the mace with specially advanced rank.

sentences.zh: 永平二年，卒，贈以本官印綬，謚曰貞侯。

translations.literal: In the second year of Yongping, he died; he was granted his original office's seals and ribbons; posthumous title was steadfast marquis.
translations.idiomatic: He died in Yongping 2 with posthumous honors at his former rank and the title Steadfast Marquis.

sentences.zh: 子躬嗣。

translations.literal: His son Gong succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: His son Yin Gong inherited the title.

sentences.zh: 躬卒，子璜嗣。

translations.literal: When Gong died, his son Huang succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Yin Gong was succeeded by his son Yin Huang.

sentences.zh: 永初七年，為奴所殺，無子，國絕。

translations.literal: In the seventh year of Yongchu, he was killed by a slave; there was no son; the state was cut off.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongchu 7 a slave murdered Yin Huang; he left no heir and the marquisate lapsed.

sentences.zh: 永寧元年，鄧太后以璜弟淑紹封。

translations.literal: In the first year of Yongning, Empress Dowager Deng used Huang's younger brother Shu to continue the enfeoffment.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongning 1 Empress Dowager Deng continued the line through Yin Huang's brother Yin Shu.

sentences.zh: 淑卒，子鮪嗣。

translations.literal: When Shu died, his son Wei succeeded.
translations.idiomatic: Yin Shu was succeeded by his son Yin Wei.

sentences.zh: 躬弟子綱女為和帝皇后，封綱吳房侯，位特進，三子秩、輔、敞，皆黃門侍郎。

translations.literal: Gong's younger brother's son Gang's daughter was Emperor He's empress; Gang was enfeoffed as marquis of Wufang, rank specially advanced; three sons Zhi, Fu, Chang, all were palace attendants of the yellow gate.
translations.idiomatic: Yin Gang's daughter became Emperor He's empress; Gang was named marquis of Wufang at specially advanced rank; his sons Zhi, Fu, and Chang became palace attendants.

sentences.zh: 後坐巫蠱事廢，綱自殺，輔下獄死，軼、敞徙日南。

translations.literal: Later he sat for the witchcraft affair and was abolished; Gang killed himself; Fu was cast in prison and died; Zhi and Chang were exiled to Rinan. (The first exile's name parallels the brother Zhi named above in the text.)
translations.idiomatic: Later the witchcraft scandal broke: Yin Gang committed suicide, Yin Fu died in prison, and his sons Zhi and Chang were exiled to Rinan.

sentences.zh: 識弟興。

translations.literal: Shi's younger brother Xing.
translations.idiomatic: Yin Shi's younger brother Yin Xing.

sentences.zh: 興字君陵，光烈皇后母弟也，為人有膂力。

translations.literal: Xing, courtesy name Junling, was Empress Guanglie's mother's younger brother; as a person he had physical strength.
translations.idiomatic: Yin Xing, courtesy name Junling, was Empress Yin's younger brother and a man of great physical strength.

sentences.zh: 建武二年，為黃門侍郎，守期門僕射，典將武騎，從征伐，平定郡國。

translations.literal: In the second year of Jianwu, he was palace attendant of the yellow gate, acting superintendent of the palace gate, in charge of leading martial cavalry; he followed in campaigns and punishments, pacifying commanderies and kingdoms.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 2 he became a palace attendant and acting superintendent of the palace gate, led the imperial cavalry on campaign, and helped pacify the realm.

sentences.zh: 興每從出入，常操持小蓋，障翳風雨，躬履塗泥，率先期門。

translations.literal: Whenever Xing followed in and out, he always held a small umbrella, screened wind and rain, personally trod mud, and led the palace gate first.
translations.idiomatic: On every outing he carried an umbrella over the emperor, waded mud, and led the palace guards.

sentences.zh: 光武所幸之處，輒先入清宮，甚見親信。

translations.literal: Wherever Guangwu favored going, he always entered first to clear the palace; he was greatly trusted.
translations.idiomatic: He always entered first to secure whatever lodging Guangwu chose and won deep trust.

sentences.zh: 雖好施接賓，然門無俠客。

translations.literal: Though he liked giving and receiving guests, yet his gate had no knight-errant clients.
translations.idiomatic: Though generous to guests, he kept no adventurers at his door.

sentences.zh: 與同郡張宗、上谷鮮于裒不相好，知其有用，猶稱所長而達之；

translations.literal: With Zhang Zong of the same commandery and Xianyu Pou of Shanggu he was not on good terms; knowing they had usefulness, he still praised their strengths and advanced them;
translations.idiomatic: He disliked Zhang Zong and Xianyu Pou but still recommended their strengths when it served the state;

sentences.zh: 友人張汜、杜禽與興厚善，以為華而少實，但私之以財，終不為言：是以世稱其忠平。

translations.literal: Friends Zhang Fan and Du Qin were thickly good with Xing; he thought them splendid but lacking substance, only privately gave them wealth, in the end did not speak for them—therefore the age called him loyal and fair.
translations.idiomatic: Friends Zhang Fan and Du Qin were close, but he thought them flashy and shallow—he gave them money privately but never recommended them. The world called him loyal and fair.

sentences.zh: 第宅茍完，裁蔽風雨。

translations.literal: His mansion was barely complete, only enough to screen wind and rain.
translations.idiomatic: His house was barely finished—only enough to keep out the weather.

sentences.zh: 九年，遷侍中，賜爵關內侯。

translations.literal: In the ninth year, he was promoted to palace attendant and granted the noble rank of full marquis within the passes.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 9 he became palace attendant and received the rank of full marquis within the passes.

sentences.zh: 帝後召興，欲封之，置印綬於前，興固讓曰：『臣未有先登陷陣之功，而一家數人並蒙爵士，令天下觖望，誠為盈溢。

translations.literal: Later the emperor summoned Xing, wished to enfeoff him, placed seals and ribbons before him; Xing firmly yielded, saying, 'Your servant has no merit of first ascending and breaching the ranks, yet several persons of one family together receive noble rank and territory—making the realm look askance—it is truly overflowing.'
translations.idiomatic: The emperor offered a fief; Yin Xing refused: 'I have never stormed the enemy first, yet our family already holds many titles—the empire would resent further reward.'

sentences.zh: 臣蒙陛下、貴人恩澤至厚，富貴已極，不可復加，至誠不願。

translations.literal: Your servant has received Your Majesty's and the honorable lady's grace and favor to the utmost; wealth and honor are already extreme, cannot be further increased—sincerely I do not wish it.'
translations.idiomatic: Your majesty and my sister have honored me enough; I cannot accept more.'

sentences.zh: 』帝嘉興之讓，不奪其志。

translations.literal: The emperor praised Xing's yielding and did not seize his intent.
translations.idiomatic: The emperor respected his refusal and did not force the title on him.

sentences.zh: 貴人問其故，興曰：『貴人不讀書心邪？

translations.literal: The honorable lady asked his reason; Xing said, 'Has the honorable lady not read the Changes?'
translations.idiomatic: His sister asked why. He said, 'Have you not read the Book of Changes?'

sentences.zh: 「亢龍有悔。

translations.literal: 'The proud dragon will have cause to repent.'
translations.idiomatic: The line on the dragon at the top of the hexagram: pride brings regret.'

sentences.zh: 」夫外戚家苦不知廉退，嫁女欲配侯王，取婦眄睨公主，愚心實不安也。

translations.literal: Now affinal families bitterly fail to know modest retreat; marrying daughters they wish to match marquises and kings; taking wives they leer at princesses—this foolish heart is truly uneasy.'
translations.idiomatic: In-law families never know when to stop—marrying daughters to princes, sons to princesses. That path terrifies me.'

sentences.zh: 富貴有極，人當知足，誇奢益為觀聽所譏。

translations.literal: Wealth and honor have a limit; people ought to know satisfaction; boasting extravagance is further mocked by sight and hearing.'
translations.idiomatic: Fortune has its limit; greed for display only draws ridicule.'

sentences.zh: 』貴人感其言，深自降挹，卒不為宗親求位。

translations.literal: The honorable lady was moved by his words, deeply lowered and restrained herself, in the end did not seek office for clan kin.'
translations.idiomatic: The empress took his words to heart, checked her family's ambition, and never sought posts for kin.'

sentences.zh: 十九年，拜衛尉，亦輔導皇太子。

translations.literal: In the nineteenth year, he was appointed superintendent of the guards, also instructing and guiding the heir apparent.
translations.idiomatic: In Jianwu 19 he became superintendent of the guards and tutored the heir apparent.

sentences.zh: 明年夏，帝風眩疾甚，後以興領侍中，受顧命於雲臺廣室。

translations.literal: The next summer, the emperor's wind-dizziness illness was severe; afterward Xing was put in charge of palace attendant, received testamentary charge in the Cloud Terrace broad chamber.
translations.idiomatic: The next summer Guangwu's vertigo grew severe; Yin Xing was named chief palace attendant and received the deathbed charge in the Cloud Terrace hall.

sentences.zh: 會疾廖，召見興，欲以代吳漢為大司馬。

translations.literal: It happened that the illness abated; he summoned and saw Xing, wished to use him to replace Wu Han as grand marshal.
translations.idiomatic: When the emperor recovered, he wished to replace Wu Han with Yin Xing as grand marshal.

sentences.zh: 興叩頭流涕，固讓曰：『臣不敢惜身，誠虧損聖德，不可茍冒。

translations.literal: Xing knocked his head and wept, firmly yielding, saying, 'Your servant does not dare spare his person; truly it would injure sagely virtue—cannot carelessly presume.'
translations.idiomatic: Yin Xing kowtowed in tears: 'I do not cling to life, but accepting would harm your reputation—I cannot.'

sentences.zh: 』至誠發中，感動左右，帝遂聽之。

translations.literal: Sincerity issued from within, moved those left and right; the emperor thereupon listened to him.'
translations.idiomatic: His sincerity moved everyone present, and the emperor yielded.'

sentences.zh: 興弟就，嗣父封宣恩侯，後改封為新陽侯。

translations.literal: Xing's younger brother Jiu succeeded to the father's enfeoffment as marquis of Xuan'en, later transferred enfeoffment to become marquis of Xinyang.
translations.idiomatic: Yin Jiu inherited their father's fief as marquis of Xuan'en, later transferred to Xinyang.

sentences.zh: 就善談論，朝臣莫及，然性剛傲，不得眾譽。

translations.literal: Jiu was good at discourse and discussion; court ministers could not match him, yet his nature was rigid and proud; he did not obtain crowd praise.
translations.idiomatic: Yin Jiu was the finest debater at court but was arrogant and unpopular.

sentences.zh: 顯宗即位，以就為少府，位特進。

translations.literal: When Emperor Ming ascended the throne, he made Jiu privy treasurer, rank specially advanced.
translations.idiomatic: Emperor Ming named him privy treasurer with specially advanced rank.

sentences.zh: 就子豐尚酈邑公主。

translations.literal: Jiu's son Feng married the Liyi princess.
translations.idiomatic: Yin Jiu's son Yin Feng married Princess Liyi.

sentences.zh: 公主嬌妒，豐亦狷急。

translations.literal: The princess was pampered and jealous; Feng was also impatient and harsh.
translations.idiomatic: The princess was jealous and spoiled; Yin Feng was hot-tempered as well.

sentences.zh: 永平二年，遂殺主，被誅，父母當坐，皆自殺，國除。

translations.literal: In the second year of Yongping, thereupon he killed the mistress, was executed, parents ought to sit for the crime—all killed themselves; the state was abolished.
translations.idiomatic: In Yongping 2 Yin Feng murdered the princess and was put to death. His parents faced collective guilt and took their own lives; the fief was abolished.

sentences.zh: 帝以舅氏故，不極其刑。

translations.literal: The emperor because of the maternal uncle clan did not carry his punishment to the limit.
translations.idiomatic: Out of regard for the maternal kin, the emperor did not extend punishment to the full extent of the law.

sentences.zh: 陰氏侯者凡四人。

translations.literal: Those of the Yin clan who were marquises were in all four persons.
translations.idiomatic: Four branches of the Yin clan held marquisates.

sentences.zh: 初，陰氏世奉管仲之祀，謂為『相君』。

translations.literal: Earlier, the Yin clan for generations offered sacrifice to Guan Zhong, calling him 'lord minister.'
translations.idiomatic: For generations the Yin family had sacrificed to Guan Zhong as 'Lord Chancellor.'

sentences.zh: 宣帝時，陰子方者，至孝有仁恩，臘日晨炊而竈神形見，子方再拜受慶。

translations.literal: Under Emperor Xuan, there was Yin Zifang—of utmost filial piety and benevolent grace; on the la sacrifice day he cooked at dawn and the stove god's form appeared; Zifang twice bowed and received blessing.
translations.idiomatic: Under Emperor Xuan, Yin Zifang was famously filial. On the la festival he lit the kitchen fire at dawn, the stove god appeared, and Zifang bowed twice to receive its blessing.

sentences.zh: 家有黃羊，因以祀之。

translations.literal: The household had a yellow sheep; thereupon with it they sacrificed to him.
translations.idiomatic: He sacrificed a yellow sheep from his flock to the god.

sentences.zh: 自是已後，暴至巨富，田有七百餘頃，輿馬僕隸，比於邦君。

translations.literal: From this after, suddenly he reached vast wealth; fields had over seven hundred qing; carriages, horses, servants, and slaves compared to a state lord.
translations.idiomatic: After that the family rose overnight to enormous wealth—more than seven hundred qing of land, carriages, horses, and retainers like a prince's household.

sentences.zh: 子方常言『我子孫必將強大』，至識三世而遂繁昌，故後常以臘日祀竈，而薦黃羊焉。

translations.literal: Zifang often said, 'My sons and grandsons will surely grow mighty'; reaching Shi, three generations, and thereupon flourished and prospered; therefore later they always on the la day sacrificed to the stove, and presented the yellow sheep.
translations.idiomatic: Zifang used to say, 'My descendants will grow powerful'—and by Yin Shi's generation, three lines later, the clan had indeed flourished. Hence the custom of sacrificing a yellow sheep to the stove on the la day.

sentences.zh: 贊曰：權族好傾，後門多毀。

translations.literal: The encomium says: Powerful clans like to topple; rear gates are often destroyed.
translations.idiomatic: Encomium: Powerful families invite ruin; back doors to power often end in disaster.

sentences.zh: 樊氏世篤，陰亦戒侈。

translations.literal: The Fan clan for generations was sincere; Yin also guarded against extravagance.
translations.idiomatic: The Fans held firm for generations; the Yins shunned excess.

sentences.zh: 恂恂苗胤，傳龜襲紫。

translations.literal: Courteous seed descendants, transmit tortoise, inherit purple.
translations.idiomatic: Their modest heirs still inherited gold seals and purple ribbons generation after generation.
